text: This is an elderly gentleman with a three-time inguinal hernia redo. He had an anatomic repair as a child, then had a direct inguinal hernia repair, and then you can see an indirect plug-and-patch repair done in the past presents with a recurrent hernia. In the office, he was very symptomatic, chronic groin pain, but had a recurrent bulge. It was difficult to tell if it was coming from the indirect repair or the direct repair in the office.

The surgeon begins dissection in the supravesicular space, extending the flap across to the contralateral side for improved manipulation and visualization. The flap is carried down towards the top of the plug.

The surgeon encounters sticky tissue medially near the vessels and continues to work laterally, pushing fat to protect the nerves. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible on the right, and suture material begins to appear in the surgical field, indicating a recurrent hernia.
The surgeon uses electrocautery and gentle manipulation to deliver the contents of the recurrent hernia, revealing additional suture material within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a camera angled thirty degrees up to visualize the surgical field, toggling between thirty up and thirty down for optimal view. The contents of the recurrent hernia are visible as they are extracted, with the dissection extending below the inguinal ligament, Cooper's ligament, and symphysis pubis, progressing back towards the hernia plug.

The surgeon approaches the mesh from above, splitting the flap to facilitate access. The mesh is split right up to the inferior epigastric vessels, allowing for dissection from medial to lateral, minimizing torque on the vessels.

The surgeon examines the plug and identifies landmarks including the inferior epigastric vessels and the spermatic cord. The flap is lifted multiple times for inspection, especially during a redo procedure. Medial dissection is performed to use as traction against the mesh being removed. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible at the top, and the surgeon dissects down to the posterior surface of the deep internal ring, ensuring no nerves are snagged during the lateral dissection.

The surgeon carefully cuts the flap to reduce tension on the vessels, improving visualization. The mesh is manipulated, ensuring it is far from critical structures. The surgeon boldly connects the dissection to the medial area and shaves the mesh away.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the spermatic cord, connecting the dissection points. The obturator plug is pulled out, with attention to avoid the nerve on the medial side. A pocket is created, and the mesh is sutured in place.

The surgeon places a high medial suture for direct hernia repair, using a triangular fixation technique with sutures positioned lateral to the chronomotor branch, medial to the iliac vessels, and at high medial and high lateral points.
